Hi, I'm Xinyuan

I'm exploring the intersection of fashion, technology, and gender — building an app that challenges how we think about clothing, identity, and sustainability on campus.

Gender Studies • App Development • AI Ethics

Read My Story
My Story
From Closet Cleanouts to Gender Research

It started with a simple observation: every semester, students throw away perfectly good clothes during move-out. I built Nudge as a campus cloth donation app to solve that waste problem.

But as I studied Gender Studies, I began seeing something deeper. The clothes we wear aren't just fabric — they're statements of identity. How we style ourselves reflects and challenges gender norms every day. And when AI systems evaluate fashion, whose standards are they using?

That question transformed Nudge from a functional app into a research platform. Now I'm analyzing how fashion trends function as a medium of gender expression and identity construction, and examining the biases hidden inside AI fashion evaluation systems.

"What if the algorithm deciding your outfit is 'stylish' is reinforcing the very stereotypes we're trying to break?"
Research
Where Fashion Meets Gender Studies

My research sits at the intersection of three fields — and asks uncomfortable questions about all of them.

👗

Fashion as Expression

Collecting and evaluating outfit images across styles and contexts to understand how design elements — color, silhouette, styling — reflect and challenge socially constructed gender norms.

🤖

AI Bias in Fashion

Designing a comparative framework to test how similar outfits are classified differently across perceived gender categories — exposing how algorithms may reinforce gender hierarchies.

🌱

Sustainable Identity

Studying how clothing donation and reuse intersect with identity expression. Can sustainable fashion be a form of resistance against gendered consumer culture?

📊

Data-Driven Analysis

Building a structured evaluation rubric to systematically analyze gender representation across fashion datasets, measuring bias in both human and AI assessments.

👥

User Perception Study

Incorporating user-based feedback to study how individuals interpret clothing in relation to their own identity — bridging the gap between algorithmic and human understanding.

⚖️

Inclusive Technology

Working to refine AI models that provide more inclusive, less biased fashion assessments — technology that respects the full spectrum of gender expression.

The App
Nudge: Campus Cloth Donation

The tool I built to reduce campus textile waste — and the platform for my gender research.

1

Photograph & Describe

Students snap a photo of clothing they want to donate, then add rich metadata — title, category, size, condition, and material composition. This structured data becomes the foundation for both the donation marketplace and my research on how clothing is categorized and perceived.

2

AI Sustainability Analysis

Each item gets an AI-powered eco-friendliness score based on material composition. The system provides a score out of 10, explains the environmental benefits of donating versus discarding, and offers care tips. This is also where I study how AI evaluates fashion — and where bias can creep in.

3

Browse & Claim

Students browse available donations with full details, AI sustainability summaries, and eco badges. Claiming is instant — no cost, no waste. Every transaction is a data point in understanding how campus communities relate to clothing, identity, and sustainability.

4

Track Your Impact

Donors track their contributions — seeing which items are still available and which have been claimed. Claims are tracked too, creating a full picture of campus clothing circulation and giving me data on how fashion moves through student communities.

App Gallery
The Full Experience
Looking Ahead
What I'm Working Toward

My next steps involve systematically expanding the dataset by curating diverse fashion images and developing structured evaluation rubrics to analyze gender representation and sustainability. I plan to incorporate user-based feedback to study how individuals interpret clothing in relation to identity, while refining the AI model to provide more inclusive and less biased assessments. The goal isn't just a better app — it's a framework for understanding how technology shapes our perception of gender through fashion.
